narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school application, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 32, and school decile rankings of 6th in Year 1 and 3rd in Year 3, while planning to attend part-time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 50% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This borderline probability tipped the decision against your application, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass confidently on their first try. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 decile ranking of 3rd, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5.53 average and signaled a potential dip in later academic momentum. On a more positive note, your part-time status actually helped your case somewhat, as it nudged the model toward expecting success compared to the vast majority of full-time students in the dataset. Similarly, your solid Year 1 decile of 6th exceeded the average and contributed positively, as did your undergraduate GPA, both suggesting strengths in your earlier performance. Your LSAT score, however, being below the typical 36.70 average, added a slight push toward the failure prediction, while family income played a minor supportive role. Overall, your profile compares to typical students as solid in early academics and background but held back by that Year 3 drop and test scores, landing you right at the 50% failure threshold rather than the stronger profiles that get approved. I know this rejection stings, especially when you've worked hard, but it's not a reflection of your full potential—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or prep, and exploring full-time options if feasible to align better with successful patterns. You might also seek targeted bar exam prep resources early or apply to programs with more flexible criteria. Keep pushing; with focused improvements on those key areas, your next application could tell a stronger story. We're rooting for you.
